title: Apache Kafka
objectives: Understand Kafka via a real-life analogy
Distinguish topics, partitions, offsets, consumer groups
Know the delivery guarantees (at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once)
Handle backpressure, retries, and dead-letter queues

sections.markdown:
**Think of Kafka as a giant append-only newspaper archive.**

- Every morning the newspaper prints new pages (events). Nobody ERASES pages — every issue is archived forever (or until retention policy purges them).
- Different DEPARTMENTS (consumer groups) read the archive at their own pace. The marketing team reads yesterday's issue while the analytics team catches up on last month. Each department bookmarks where they are (**offset**). If someone new joins, they start from wherever they want — beginning, latest, or a specific date.
- The newspaper is split into SECTIONS (**partitions**) — Sports, Business, Local — so multiple staff can print in parallel. Each section is strictly ORDERED inside itself (Business page 1 comes before page 2), but sections don't order across each other.
- **Critical:** you can read the same event MANY TIMES with MANY consumers. Kafka doesn't 'consume' data — reading doesn't delete it.

**In code:** Kafka is a **distributed commit log**. Producers append events to TOPICS; topics split into PARTITIONS for parallelism; each partition is a strictly-ordered, immutable sequence. Consumers read from partitions at their own pace, tracking their position (offset). Multiple consumer GROUPS can read the same topic independently.

**Why it dominates:** LinkedIn built Kafka (2011) for high-throughput event streaming. Now it powers Netflix, Uber, Airbnb, LinkedIn — millions of events per second. **Use cases:** event sourcing, streaming analytics, log aggregation, decoupling microservices (producer doesn't know consumers), CDC (change data capture from DBs), audit logs.

**Trade-off vs RabbitMQ:** Kafka is optimized for VOLUME + REPLAY (a stream you can rewind). RabbitMQ is optimized for TASK QUEUES (one worker processes each message; retry semantics; complex routing). Choose based on the workload — see next topic.

**Apache Kafka** is a distributed streaming platform originally from LinkedIn (2011, open-sourced 2012). Data model: **topics** (named streams) split into **partitions** (ordered logs); **producers** append records; **consumers** read records at their own pace tracking **offsets**; **consumer groups** distribute partitions among multiple consumers for parallelism. Storage is IMMUTABLE APPEND-ONLY — reading doesn't delete. Retention configurable (7 days default; can be forever). Replicated for HA (each partition has a leader + N followers).
## The five core concepts

rows: Ordered subset of a topic; unit of parallelism
One SECTION of the newspaper (Sports, Business)
One or more consumers sharing partition work
A department reading; each member takes some partitions

sections.markdown: ## Partitions + consumer groups — the parallelism model

sections.code:
Topic `orders` with 4 partitions:

    partition 0: [ record1, record2, record3, ... ]   ← strictly ordered
    partition 1: [ record4, record5, record6, ... ]
    partition 2: [ record7, record8, record9, ... ]
    partition 3: [ record10, ...                  ]

Producer routes records to partitions:
  - By KEY hash: same key ALWAYS goes to same partition (order within key).
    Perfect for 'all events for user 42 in order' — key = userId.
  - Round-robin: no key, distribute evenly.

Consumer group 'billing-service' (3 consumers):

    consumer A → partitions 0, 3      (Kafka assigns)
    consumer B → partition 1
    consumer C → partition 2

Each partition assigned to EXACTLY ONE consumer in the group.
More consumers than partitions? Extras are idle.
More partitions than consumers? Each consumer takes several.

Same topic, different group 'analytics-service':
  Reads the SAME records independently, at its own pace.
  Doesn't affect billing-service.

Rule: number of partitions = MAX parallelism for a consumer group.
  Under-partition = bottleneck. Over-partition = overhead. Rule of thumb:
  target ~1000 partitions per Kafka cluster; more per topic for high-throughput
  ones; often start with 6-12.

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — Spring Boot Kafka producer + consumer
## Delivery guarantees — pick your semantics

rows: Fast; loses records if consumer crashes mid-process
Records may replay on retry; consumer MUST be idempotent

sections.markdown: **The senior take:** true exactly-once semantics work WITHIN Kafka (read from topic A → process → write to topic B, all atomic). Across systems (Kafka → external API → DB), you CAN'T get exactly-once for free — you must make the external side idempotent. Assume at-least-once; make consumers idempotent (Redis SET NX, DB unique constraint, event-id dedup table). That gives you EFFECTIVE exactly-once behavior with much simpler code.
## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: Auto-commit offsets before processing.** `enable.auto.commit=true` — messages are marked processed before your code runs. Consumer crashes = data lost. Fix: manual ack after processing.
**Mistake 2: Consumers not idempotent.** Redeliveries → duplicate charges, duplicate emails. Fix: dedup by event id in Redis or a DB table.
**Mistake 3: Under-partitioning.** Topic has 1 partition, 10 consumers — 9 idle. Fix: choose partition count based on target throughput; increase before need.
**Mistake 4: Over-partitioning.** 10,000 partitions per topic → controller overhead, slow recoveries. Rule of thumb: ~1,000 partitions per cluster; per-topic scaled to throughput.
**Mistake 5: Producer without `acks=all` in production.** `acks=1` risks data loss on leader failure. `acks=all` waits for replicas — safer, slightly slower.
**Mistake 6: No dead-letter queue.** Poison messages block the consumer forever. Fix: after N retries, publish to `topic.dlt`; alert; investigate offline.
**Mistake 7: Consumer lag ignored.** Your consumer is 4 hours behind → users get stale data. Monitor lag; alert on it.
## Best practices
**1. Manual offset commits AFTER processing.** `enable-auto-commit: false` + explicit `ack.acknowledge()`.
**2. Idempotent consumers.** Dedup by event id in Redis (SET NX) or DB unique constraint.
**3. `acks=all` + `enable.idempotence: true` on producers.** Prevents duplicate writes on retry.
**4. Partition key = business grouping.** userId, orderId, tenantId — preserves order within that entity.
**5. Dead-letter queue.** After N retries, park the message; alert; investigate later.
**6. Schema registry (Avro / Protobuf).** Contract-check between producer and consumer at build time.
**7. Monitor consumer lag.** Alert if group lag > threshold.
**8. Kafka Streams / ksqlDB for stream processing.** Don't hand-roll windowing + state.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Local Kafka via docker-compose.** Bitnami Kafka + Kafka UI; produce + consume via `kafka-console-producer` / `-consumer`.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Spring Boot producer + consumer.** Build the sample; verify partition assignment by key.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Consumer group scaling.** Start 3 consumers; watch partition assignment rebalance.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Idempotent processing.** Simulate a redelivery; verify Redis SET NX prevents double-charge.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Dead-letter queue.** Throw an exception; verify message moves to `orders.dlt` after N retries.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Kafka Streams.** Windowed aggregation: 5-minute rolling total per user, output to `user-totals` topic.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Kafka and what problem does it solve?**

**A:** Kafka is a distributed streaming platform — a **log** where producers append events and multiple consumer groups read at their own pace. **Problems it solves:** (1) **Decoupling** — producer doesn't know consumers; add new consumers anytime. (2) **Volume + durability** — millions of events/sec, retained for days/weeks/forever. (3) **Replay** — a new service can process last month's events by resetting its offset. (4) **Backpressure** — slow consumer just falls behind (offset lag); doesn't kill the producer. **Compare:** RabbitMQ is a task queue (one worker processes each message once, then it's gone). Kafka is a log (many consumers replay). Both matter; different jobs. Kafka's use cases: event sourcing, streaming analytics, log aggregation, microservice event bus, CDC.

**Q2: What are partitions and consumer groups?**

**A:** A **partition** is an ordered, immutable log — the unit of parallelism inside a topic. A topic with 4 partitions can be consumed by up to 4 consumers in a single group in parallel. **Ordering** is guaranteed WITHIN a partition (records 1, 2, 3 in order) but NOT across partitions. **Producers** choose partition via key hash (`hash(userId) % 4`) — all events for one key go to the same partition, preserving per-user order. **A consumer group** is a set of consumers sharing partition assignments. If group `billing-service` has 4 consumers reading a 4-partition topic, each consumer gets one partition. Add a 5th consumer → it sits idle. Multiple GROUPS read the same topic INDEPENDENTLY — `billing-service` and `analytics-service` each track their own offsets, don't interfere.

**Q3: What are Kafka's delivery guarantees?**

**A:** **At most once** — consumer commits offset BEFORE processing. Fast; loses records if consumer crashes mid-process. Rare in production. **At least once** (default) — commit AFTER processing. Records may replay if the consumer crashes between processing and committing. Consumer MUST be idempotent. **Exactly once** — Kafka Transactions (Kafka 0.11+) + idempotent producer + `isolation.level=read_committed`. Truly one-and-only-once WITHIN Kafka. **Reality:** exactly-once works within Kafka (topic A → process → topic B, atomic). Across systems (Kafka → REST API), you CAN'T get free exactly-once — make consumers idempotent. **Rule:** assume at-least-once; make consumers idempotent (Redis SET NX, DB unique constraint on event id). Effective exactly-once with much simpler code.

**Q4: How does Kafka guarantee message ordering?**

**A:** Ordering is **guaranteed within a single partition**. Records go into the log in append order; consumers read them in that same order. **NOT guaranteed across partitions** — records in partition 0 don't have an ordering relative to partition 1. **To preserve order per business key** (e.g., all events for user 42 in order): use userId as the KEY on the producer. Kafka partitions by `hash(key) % numPartitions` — same key → same partition → strict order for that user. **Consequence:** if you need global ordering, use one partition (limits throughput). If you need per-entity ordering, key by entity id. **Rule:** design your partition key to match the ordering boundary you actually need. Most business use cases only need per-user or per-order ordering, which keys give you.

**Q5: How do you make a Kafka consumer idempotent?**

**A:** The consumer is called MULTIPLE times for the same event (retries, rebalances). Idempotency means side effects happen at most once. **Techniques:** **(1) Redis SET NX with event id.** `SETNX processed:orders:42 1 EX 604800` — atomically claim; second call is a no-op. **(2) DB unique constraint on event id.** INSERT fails on duplicate; catch and ignore. **(3) UPSERT with conditional update.** `UPDATE orders SET status = 'CHARGED' WHERE id = 42 AND status = 'PENDING'` — no-op if already charged. **(4) Idempotency key from producer.** Producer generates a UUID per business event; consumer dedups on it. **Rule:** every side effect (charge card, send email, insert row) needs its own idempotency mechanism. Kafka gives you replay; your code guarantees safety on replay.

**Q6: Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs Redis Streams?**

**A:** **Kafka** — high-volume STREAMING log. Replayable. Multiple consumer groups. Rich ecosystem (Streams, Connect, ksqlDB). Ops-heavy (ZooKeeper or KRaft). Best for: event sourcing, analytics, CDC, microservice event bus. **RabbitMQ** — traditional TASK QUEUE. Complex routing (exchanges + bindings). One worker gets each message (consume = remove). Best for: job queues, RPC, complex routing, low-latency. **Redis Streams** — Kafka-like log inside Redis. Consumer groups, XCLAIM for stuck messages. Lower operational overhead but limited scale (single Redis or Cluster). Best for: modest-volume streaming when you already have Redis. **Rule:** Kafka for streaming + replay + high volume; RabbitMQ for task queues + complex routing; Redis Streams for smaller-scale streaming with less ops. Don't pick Kafka for a job queue; don't pick RabbitMQ for event sourcing.

**Q7: What's a Dead Letter Queue and why do you need one?**

**A:** A **Dead Letter Queue (DLT / DLQ)** holds messages that failed processing after N retries. Without one: poison message blocks the consumer FOREVER (retries every second, never succeeds, no progress on new messages). With a DLQ: after N attempts, the message goes to `orders.dlt`; consumer moves on; humans investigate the DLQ offline. **Implementation in Spring Kafka:** `DefaultErrorHandler` + `DeadLetterPublishingRecoverer` — auto-retry with backoff, then publish to a `.dlt` topic on final failure. **Monitor the DLQ** — alerts on non-empty DLQ. **Recovery** — replay from DLQ after fixing the bug. **Alternative:** parking lot topic + manual analyst tool. **Rule:** every production consumer has a DLQ. Without it, one bad message ruins your morning.

## Real-world usage

- **LinkedIn (creator), Netflix, Uber, Airbnb, Twitter** — millions of events/sec each.
- **Confluent (managed Kafka)** — the SaaS.
- **AWS MSK (Managed Streaming for Kafka)** — the cloud offering.
- **Kafka Connect** — CDC connectors for Postgres/MySQL (Debezium), sinks to S3/BigQuery/Elasticsearch.
- **Kafka Streams** — stream processing library.
- **ksqlDB** — SQL over Kafka streams.
- **Change Data Capture** — Debezium reads DB write-ahead logs, publishes to Kafka; the standard pattern for reliable event publishing (Outbox alternative).
- **Not for:** RPC (too slow), request/response (wrong model), low-volume simple queues (RabbitMQ).

## Advanced notes

- **KRaft mode** (Kafka 3.0+) — removes ZooKeeper; Kafka manages its own metadata via Raft.
- **Tiered storage** — hot tier on local disk, cold tier on S3.
- **Compacted topics** — deduplication by key; keeps only the latest value per key (like a KV store).
- **Exactly-once semantics (EOS)** — `transactional.id` producer + read_committed consumer.
- **Interceptor API** — instrument produce/consume for tracing.
- **MirrorMaker 2** — cross-cluster replication.
- **Schema Registry** — Avro/Protobuf/JSON schemas with backward-compat enforcement.
- **Confluent Cloud, AWS MSK, Aiven** — managed Kafka services.
- **`kcat` (formerly kafkacat)** — CLI for producing/consuming.
- **`kafka-ui`, Conduktor** — GUIs.
- **JMX metrics** — exposed on every broker for monitoring.
- **Consumer lag monitoring** — Burrow, LinkedIn's monitor.
- **Kafka Connect** — declarative source + sink connectors (Debezium for CDC, S3 sink, Elasticsearch sink).
- **Streams API** — stateful stream processing with KTable + KStream + windowing.

flashcards.back: Distributed append-only log; topics split into ordered partitions; consumers read at their own pace tracking offsets.
Within a partition, yes. Across partitions, no. Key by business id (userId) to preserve per-entity order.
Set of consumers sharing partition assignments. Each partition assigned to exactly one consumer in the group.
Redis SET NX event id, DB unique constraint, conditional UPDATE, producer-supplied idempotency key.
After N retries, publish to `topic.dlt` to unblock the consumer; investigate offline.
Kafka = streaming log with replay. RabbitMQ = task queue (consume removes).

quiz.explain: One partition per consumer in a group.

options: Key by userId (hash to same partition)

challenges.text: Local Kafka via docker-compose; produce + consume via CLI.
Key by userId; verify same user always same partition.
Consumer group scaling: 3 consumers on 6 partitions; watch rebalance.
Idempotent consumer with Redis SET NX; verify replay is safe.
Dead-letter topic on N-retry failure with @RetryableTopic.
Kafka Streams: 5-minute rolling total per user, output to `user-totals`.
